title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a mycologist called Ryan Miller talking about the potential of fungi to solve environmental problems. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a mycologist called Ryan Miller talking about the potential of fungi to solve environmental problems. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ryan Miller discusses how mushrooms and mycelium can be used in manufacturing and waste management.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Ryan. For most people, fungi are either a topping on a pizza or something that ruins the bread in the cupboard. But to a scientist, they are the most versatile organisms on Earth. My fascination with this kingdom didn't start in a lab; it actually began when I found a [7]giant puffball mushroom[/7] in the woods as a child. I was amazed at how something so large could grow overnight. I wanted to understand that explosive growth.

We are now discovering that fungi can actually 'eat' our waste. I’ve found that certain species are capable of [8]breaking down plastic[/8] in a matter of weeks, rather than the centuries it takes in a landfill. They produce enzymes that digest the complex polymers and turn them into organic matter. It’s a completely natural solution to one of our biggest environmental crises.

Fungi are also changing the construction industry. We are now 'growing' bricks using mycelium—the root-like structure of a fungus. I noticed that [9]agricultural waste[/9], like corn husks or sawdust, is the perfect fuel for this process. We put the waste into a mold, add the mycelium, and in a few days, it becomes a solid, fire-resistant block. It’s a zero-waste material that could eventually replace polystyrene in packaging.

In the world of fashion, 'mushroom leather' is becoming a reality. I’ve found that [10]textile manufacturers[/10] are increasingly interested in these sustainable alternatives. It feels like real leather, but it uses a fraction of the water and land. Plus, it’s completely biodegradable. You could essentially compost your old shoes at the end of their life.

Technology is helping us map the 'Wood Wide Web'. This is the underground network of fungi that allows trees to communicate. I noticed that [11]nutrient exchange[/11] between different species of trees is managed by these fungal threads. If one tree is struggling, the others can send it resources through the mycelium. It’s a level of cooperation that we never thought possible in nature.

However, we have to protect these networks. I’m worried that [12]intensive farming[/12] is destroying the soil’s fungal diversity. When we use too many pesticides, we break the connections that keep the forest healthy. We need to rethink how we manage our land before we lose these invisible support systems.

Looking forward, I believe we will see 'living clothes' made from fungi. I noticed that [13]self-repairing fabrics[/13] are already being tested in laboratories. Imagine a jacket that could heal itself if it got a tear, just like a living organism. It’s the ultimate sustainable solution for the fashion industry.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]field identification app[/14] that uses AI to help amateur foragers identify mushrooms safely. I want to encourage people to explore the world of fungi without the risk of accidental poisoning. Thank you.
